questions.text: I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that most powerfully demonstrates the link between what I believe and what I do.
I eat when I'm hungry and exercise when I feel like it, without a fixed schedule.
I often learn about social conflicts well after they have already been resolved.
I notice when someone is being left out of a conversation even when it is not obvious.
I rarely set alarms or reminders; I trust my natural sense of timing.
I am recognized for my ability to weave diverse viewpoints into a cohesive group effort.
I design my environment to support my goals, removing temptations and distractions.
I feel that my savings account is also an emergency fund for the people I care about.
I rarely sense when a group has an unspoken agreement about who the designated leader is.
I often catch the quick glance two people exchange when something significant is said.
I find that arguments or confrontations don't leave me shaken afterward.
I feel that buying the best tools and equipment for myself directly improves my output.
I tend to buy gifts for others before buying things I need for myself.
I break large projects into smaller milestones with specific deadlines.
I feel that a reliable person is a valuable person.
I detect when someone's goodbye carries finality versus when it is a casual see-you-later.
I find that organizing events for others brings me more joy than attending events for myself.
I find that my emotional bandwidth is wide enough to handle most things without feeling stretched.
I tend to blur territorial lines in favor of fluid collaboration.
I believe that the capacity for dispassionate evaluation of human need is what makes the most impactful charitable and professional work possible.
When I'm moved by something, the feeling washes over me like a wave.
I consider it part of my job to mentor others by directing their growth.
When I'm upset, the feeling saturates my entire body.
I feel that my directness helps groups move forward.
I rarely notice when someone is trying to impress a particular person in a group.
I believe that I function best when I have dedicated, uninterrupted time for my own work.
I feel that the moral weight of another's pain is the one consideration that reliably overrides all others in my decision-making.
I hold the view that attending to my own emotional well-being should come before attending to anyone else's.
I find that being in nature during an emotional state amplifies the feeling rather than dampening it.
I believe that the most sustainable forms of care and service are those delivered from a position of emotional stability rather than emotional immersion.
I hold the view that emotional intensity is a spectrum, and I simply sit at the calmer end of it.
I tend to make executive decisions about scope changes mid-project.
I feel that the moral weight of suffering should inform my actions regardless of whether acting is convenient or expected.
I believe that processing suffering as data to be understood rather than emotion to be surrendered to is the key to making well-informed decisions.
I feel that a life full of surprises and variety is a rich life.
I find myself naturally framing situations as challenges for us to solve together.
